channel.title: Los Angeles Review of Books – Reviews
channel.description: Book reviews from the Los Angeles Review of Books.

item.title: Murky Effects
item.description: A novel of desire and domestic disorder amid a disintegrating literary establishment.

item.title: The Embodiment of Lena Dunham
item.description: Lena Dunham’s memoir shows the distorting effects of fame on her intimate relationships, especially with herself.

item.title: Competing Visions of China’s Distant Past
item.description: Through examinations of ecological impacts or material objects, two books about China’s ancient past offer very different perspectives on its place in the world today.

item.title: Man out of Time
item.description: The travels and ecstasies of a Russian aesthete.

item.title: Going Back (Again) to the East Village
item.description: Natalie Adler’s debut novel depicts the haunting transition of New York’s East Village during the AIDS crisis, from queer avant-garde to gentrified graveyard.

item.title: California Sons: Miriam Pawel’s “The Browns of California”
item.description: Jake Wertz reviews “The Browns of California,” a four-generation biography of the Brown family and a portrait of the state they shaped.

item.title: A Life Less Governed by Death
item.description: Ecuadoran author Mónica Ojeda’s new novel is a celebration of the transfiguring power of dance, music, and neo-shamanistic knowledge.

item.title: Only Fact
item.description: Karen Solie’s new collection explores how to live with nature, rather than against it.

item.title: A Museum Gone to Pot
item.description: Rose B. Simpson parks lowriders inside the de Young Museum in San Francisco.

item.title: The Narratives We Need
item.description: Samantha Mills’s stories hold a mirror up to our society and reveal all the ways it is deeply and fundamentally broken.

item.title: The Failure of Bildungsroman
item.description: In Indonesian author Eka Kurniawan’s new novel, crime and its punishment are the ultimate grammar of collectivity.

item.title: Story Is Haunting
item.description: Ali Smith’s new novel is less a story than a meditation on story.

item.title: This Explains Everything
item.description: The Grateful Dead, professional wrestling, and the vertigo of the pop culture microhistory.
